nativeRange.habitat: Large rivers and their tributaries; mid-water column in open flowing water over sandy or rocky substrates; also found in flooded areas during wet season
nativeRange.biotope: Mekong river system — large tropical rivers with moderate to strong current, clear to slightly turbid water, sandy and rocky substrates

decorPreferences: open mid-water swimming space (schooling species requires room for group movement)
large smooth river stones and boulders
coarse sand or fine gravel substrate
minimal or no live plants (will be destroyed or ignored)
moderate water flow replicating Mekong river conditions
caves or overhangs as retreat options

diet.notes: The Golden Puffer feeds primarily on smaller fish, invertebrates, and molluscs in the wild. In captivity, many specimens initially refuse anything other than live feeder fish — small livebearers or minnows are commonly used to trigger a feeding response. With patience, some individuals can be weaned onto frozen and thawed whole fish such as lance fish and whitebait, along with large frozen bloodworm, prawns, mussels, and cockles. Hard-shelled prey — snails and mussels served in the shell — should be offered regularly, as the beak-like teeth can overgrow without adequate abrasion from crunching hard foods. Soft foods alone are not sufficient. Earthworms and market prawns are accepted by some specimens. Adults are generally fed three to four times per week.

goodWith: conspecifics (Auriglobus silus) in groups of 8 or more — species-only setup strongly recommended
very large, robust fast-moving fish of similar or greater size if a species-only setup is not possible (not generally recommended)
avoidWith: any fish smaller than the puffer (will be eaten)
invertebrates — shrimp, snails, and crabs will be consumed
conspecific groups fewer than 8 (inter-group aggression intensifies in small groups, leading to injuries and deaths)
fish with delicate fins or body structure

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species photo page for Auriglobus silus (species ID 13147). Check for available images showing the characteristic golden-iridescent body coloration.
Search Wikimedia Commons for Auriglobus silus; limited images may be available given the species’ relative obscurity in the hobby.

summary: The Golden Puffer (Auriglobus silus) is a rare schooling puffer from the Mekong basin, reaching 15 cm and requiring groups of 8 or more in a species-only tank of 500 L or more. Its golden-iridescent body distinguishes it from the superficially similar Schoutedeni Puffer of the Congo basin.
careNotes: The Golden Puffer is one of the most unusual members of the puffer family, being a true schooling species rather than the typically solitary or territorial puffer. Despite this social nature, it is widely considered an extreme piscivore — fish smaller than the puffer are at high risk of being eaten, making a species-only setup of 8 or more conspecifics the only practical approach. Tank size should be no less than 500 L with a footprint that allows the school to move freely; 600–800 L is preferable for groups of 8–10. Water flow should be moderate to replicate Mekong river conditions; high filtration is essential given the heavy bioload of large carnivores. Temperature range 24–28°C; pH slightly acidic to neutral (6.5–7.5). Diet poses the primary husbandry challenge: most specimens available in the trade are wild-caught, and many arrive feeding only on live fish. Patient weaning onto frozen foods using feeding tongs — starting with live feeders and gradually substituting frozen whitebait, lance fish, or defrosted whole prawns — is generally necessary. Hard-shelled prey such as snails and mussels in the shell must be provided periodically to prevent dental overgrowth; puffer teeth do not wear sufficiently on soft foods alone and may require veterinary trimming in extreme cases. Inter-conspecific aggression, while reduced in groups of 8 or more, remains meaningful: watch for persistent fin damage on subordinate individuals and have contingency holding capacity available. Groups smaller than 8 tend to experience intense hierarchical aggression. Copper-based medications are tolerated poorly by puffers as a family; copper-free disease treatments should be used instead. The species is sometimes confused at the point of sale with the Schoutedeni Puffer (Tetraodon schoutedeni) from the Democratic Republic of Congo — verify the scientific name before purchase.
breedingNotes: No captive breeding of Auriglobus silus has been documented. Spawning behaviour in the wild is not well described in the aquarium literature. Given the logistical challenges of maintaining a sufficiently large school of large piscivores in captivity, captive breeding is considered impractical for home aquarists and has not been reported in the hobby.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Auriglobus silus
primary.notes: Primary husbandry reference. Provided native range (Mekong basin: Thailand, Cambodia, Vietnam, Laos), adult size (to 15 cm), water parameters, diet (piscivore/molluscivore), schooling behaviour (minimum group size 8+), and species-only tank recommendation. Confirmed Tetraodontidae/Tetraodontiformes taxonomy.

additional.name: FishBase — Auriglobus silus
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Tetraodontidae, Tetraodontiformes), adult size (max ~15 cm TL), native range (Mekong River basin), and diet (carnivore/piscivore). Confirmed species is distinct from Auriglobus modestus and Auriglobus nefastus within the genus.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Freshwater Puffer Fish Care
additional.notes: General freshwater puffer care guide providing trade context: feeding difficulty (live foods, tooth maintenance), copper sensitivity in puffers, and the general challenge of puffer husbandry for aquarists. Auriglobus silus not individually profiled; general puffer guidance applied.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Freshwater Puffers
additional.notes: Provided context on schooling behaviour of Auriglobus genus puffers, large tank requirements, species-only setup rationale, and distinction from African puffer species including Tetraodon schoutedeni. Noted golden iridescence as identifying characteristic.

additional.name: TFH Magazine — Puffer Fish of the Mekong
additional.notes: Supplementary source on Mekong basin biotope, natural schooling behaviour in Auriglobus silus, piscivorous diet, and husbandry notes on large-group maintenance and live-feeder dependence.

fish.breedingNotes: No documented captive breeding. Wild-caught specimens dominate the trade. Spawning conditions unknown; considered impractical for home aquarists given the large school size and tank volume required.

hobbyistAdvisory.reason: Aggressive predatory schooling puffer reaching 15cm; needs 500L+ tank with conspecific-only setup (8+ specimens). Live-feeder dependent; high inter-conspecific aggression in smaller groups.
